stem: A group of students were asked which of three activities they enjoyed: Cycling, Swimming and Running.
question: Which statement best describes people who enjoyed exactly two activities?

options.text: People who enjoyed any two activities, including those who enjoyed all three.
People who enjoyed two activities but not the third.
People who enjoyed at least one activity.
People who enjoyed only one activity.

explanation: Exactly two means membership in two sets but not the third. Anyone who enjoyed all three is excluded.

review.whySafeToInclude: This tests precise UCAT wording for exactly two versus at least two or any two including all three.

stem: A group were asked how they like their eggs cooked. 14 liked boiled, 11 liked scrambled and 12 liked fried. 6 liked all three ways. 3 liked exactly two ways. 2 liked none of them.
question: How many people were asked?
explanation: Total mentions = 14 + 11 + 12 = 37. All-three mentions = 6 × 3 = 18. Exactly-two mentions = 3 × 2 = 6. That leaves 37 − 18 − 6 = 13 people who liked exactly one way. Total people = 13 + 3 + 6 + 2 = 24.

review.whySafeToInclude: The stem separates all three, exactly two and none. The total follows from mention counting.

stem: There are 48 people in a room. 29 say they like reading. 9 say they like watching television.
question: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: Between 10 and 19 inclusive people like neither reading nor television.
Exactly 20 people like reading but not television.
More people do not like reading than do not like watching television.
Exactly 38 people like either reading or television.

explanation: At most 29 + 9 = 38 like reading or television, so at least 48 − 38 = 10 like neither. At least 29 like reading (all television fans could be readers), so at most 48 − 29 = 19 like neither. The number who like neither must be between 10 and 19 inclusive.
optional_working_steps: Maximum reading or TV = 29 + 9 = 38, so minimum neither = 10.
Minimum reading or TV = 29, so maximum neither = 19.

review.whySafeToInclude: Overlap is unknown, so only a range for neither can be proved. Other options fix exact counts without justification.

stem: In Millbrook, half of the 200 homes have both a front garden and a back garden. 70 homes do not have a back garden. 85 homes do not have a front garden.
question: How many homes have neither a front garden nor a back garden?
explanation: 100 homes have both gardens. Let x be the number with neither. Homes with only a front garden = 70 − x. Homes with only a back garden = 85 − x. So (70 − x) + 100 + (85 − x) + x = 200, giving 255 − x = 200 and x = 55.

review.whySafeToInclude: The not-a wording is translated into only-front and only-back regions. The equation has one solution.

stem: Members were asked which keep-fit methods they used: Cycling, Swimming and Running. No member used all three. 12 used exactly two methods. 10 used only Running. 9 used both Cycling and Swimming.
question: How many members used Running?
explanation: Exactly two includes the 9 who used both Cycling and Swimming, so 12 − 9 = 3 used Running as one of exactly two other pairs. Total using Running = only Running + those two Running pairs = 10 + 3 = 13.

review.whySafeToInclude: No all-three region is stated. Exactly two is partitioned using the given Cycling and Swimming pair.

stem: Route B has a 60% likelihood of remaining congestion free.
question: What is the likelihood of congestion on Route B?
explanation: Congestion and congestion-free are complements, so they add to 100%. Congestion = 100% − 60% = 40%.

review.whySafeToInclude: Single complement step with whole-number percentages.

stem: 150 patients reported exactly one post-operative complication. 36% reported breathing issues, 45 reported cardiovascular complaints and 18% reported hypothermia. The remaining patients reported nausea.
question: What percentage of patients reported nausea?
explanation: 45 out of 150 is 30%. Known percentages are 36%, 30% and 18%, totalling 84%. The remaining share is 100% − 84% = 16%.

review.whySafeToInclude: Exactly one complication makes categories mutually exclusive. The remaining percentage is determined uniquely.

stem: Survey X involved 200 people. 84 said they liked the product, 76 said they did not like the product. The rest did not respond.
question: What percentage of those who responded liked the product?
explanation: Respondents = 84 + 76 = 160. Of those who responded, 84 liked the product. 84 ÷ 160 × 100 = 52.5%.

review.whySafeToInclude: The question specifies respondents as the denominator, not everyone surveyed.

stem: Kai takes two flights. Seat assignment is independent on each flight. The probability of a window seat on each flight is 0.4.
question: Is Kai more likely than not to get at least one window seat?

options.text: Yes, because the probability of at least one window seat is 0.64.
Yes, because the probability of at least one window seat is 0.8.
No, because the probability of window seats on both flights is only 0.16.
No, because the probability of at least one window seat is 0.24.

explanation: Use the complement. No window on one flight: 0.6. No window on both: 0.6 × 0.6 = 0.36. At least one window: 1 − 0.36 = 0.64, which is greater than 0.5.

review.whySafeToInclude: Independence is stated. Complement method gives a single numeric answer.

stem: X and Y take turns throwing a biased six-sided die. The first person to throw a six wins. X throws first.
question: Do X and Y have an equal chance of winning?

options.text: Yes, because they use the same die on each turn.
Yes, because bias does not change the probability of a six on a single throw.
No, because X has a chance to win before Y gets a turn.
No, but only if the die is biased towards six.

explanation: Y can only win if X fails first. X has the first opportunity to throw a six, so X has the advantage regardless of how the die is biased.

review.whySafeToInclude: Turn order creates first-throw advantage. Bias magnitude does not remove that structural edge.

stem: To improve children's health, should all primary-age children be given free school lunches?
question: Select the strongest argument.

options.text: Yes, school lunches may help children concentrate in class.
options.label: true-but-irrelevant

options.text: Yes, free school lunches can save carers money over a year.
options.label: true-but-irrelevant

options.text: Yes, school lunches can help support good eating habits in children.
options.label: directly-relevant

options.text: No, a lot of school lunches are wasted each year.
options.label: does-not-answer-aim

explanation: The aim is improving children's health. Good eating habits link most directly to health. Concentration, cost and waste do not address that aim as directly.

review.whyCorrect: C links school lunches to good eating habits, which links to health.
review.whyBIsWrong: B discusses saving money, not health.
review.whyDIsWrong: D discusses waste, not a health benefit from lunches.
review.whySafeToInclude: The aim is explicit and only one option targets health most directly.

stem: To reduce costs, increase accuracy and improve turnout, should voting in elections be undertaken online or, exceptionally, by post?
question: Select the strongest argument.

options.text: Yes, online or postal voting would be more convenient and would therefore be likely to improve turnout.
options.label: only-addresses-one-criterion

options.text: Yes, electronic counting would provide results more rapidly and with less chance of error.
options.label: only-addresses-one-criterion

options.text: No, the necessary equipment would be expensive, electronic voting may discourage some voters, and hackers might interfere with the result.
options.label: directly-relevant

options.text: No, current systems work effectively, so there is no need to change.
options.label: vague

explanation: The stem lists cost, accuracy and turnout. C addresses all three: equipment cost, discouraging voters and hacking affecting accuracy. A mainly addresses turnout. B mainly addresses accuracy. D is vague.

review.exactAim: reduce costs, increase accuracy and improve turnout
review.whyCorrect: C responds to cost, turnout and accuracy in one argument.
review.whyAIsWrong: A focuses on convenience and turnout only.
review.whyBIsWrong: B focuses on speed and accuracy only.
review.whyDIsWrong: D does not engage with the three criteria.
review.whySafeToInclude: Three criteria are explicit. Only C addresses all of them.

stem: Should society rather than family members be ultimately responsible for the care of older people when they can no longer mentally or physically care for themselves?
question: Select the strongest argument.

options.text: Yes, family members may help where possible, but society should ensure care is available when family cannot provide it.
options.label: directly-relevant

options.text: Yes, older people with mental illness can be difficult for untrained relatives to support.
options.label: too-narrow

options.text: No, families should learn to stick together by caring for relatives themselves.
options.label: partially-relevant

options.text: No, everyone should save enough money to pay for their own care.
options.label: unsupported-assumption

explanation: A answers who should be ultimately responsible and covers cases where families cannot provide care. B only discusses mental illness. C does not solve cases where families cannot help. D assumes everyone can save enough.

review.exactAim: who should ultimately be responsible for care when older people cannot care for themselves
review.whyCorrect: A balances family help with society's ultimate responsibility when family care is not possible.
review.whyBIsWrong: B is too narrow because it only discusses mental illness.
review.whyCIsWrong: C does not address inability of families to provide care.
review.whyDIsWrong: D relies on the unsupported assumption that everyone can save enough.
review.whySafeToInclude: A matches the full scope of the stem. Wrong options fail on scope or assumptions.

stem: Will enforcing uniforms in the workplace boost productivity?
question: Select the strongest argument.

options.text: Yes, shared workwear may promote team identity, and stronger team identity can improve workplace performance.
options.label: directly-relevant

options.text: Yes, all employees will save time every morning if they are required to wear a uniform.
options.label: unsupported-assumption

options.text: Yes, uniforms will remove every form of inequality in the workplace.
options.label: overclaim

options.text: No, uniforms are always unpopular with employees.
options.label: unsupported-assumption

explanation: A links uniforms to team identity and then to performance. B assumes all employees lose significant choosing time that becomes productive work. C overclaims. D is an unsupported generalisation.

review.exactAim: whether workplace uniforms will boost productivity
review.whyCorrect: A gives a direct chain from uniforms to team identity to performance.
review.whyBIsWrong: B assumes uniform time savings become productive output for all staff.
review.whyCIsWrong: C claims uniforms remove every form of inequality.
review.whyDIsWrong: D makes a blanket claim about popularity without linking to productivity.
review.whySafeToInclude: Only A links uniforms to productivity with a plausible mechanism.

stem: In order to reduce fatal motoring accidents, should cars be fitted with a device to limit the speeds at which they can travel?
question: Select the strongest argument.

options.text: Yes, many deaths are caused by drivers who break speed limits.
options.label: directly-relevant

options.text: Yes, driving at fast speeds uses more fuel and increases pollution.
options.label: true-but-irrelevant

options.text: No, people should decide for themselves how fast they drive.
options.label: does-not-answer-aim

options.text: No, more road deaths are caused by drink driving than by speeding.
options.label: true-but-irrelevant

explanation: The aim is reducing fatal accidents. A links speed-limit breaking to deaths and supports limiting speed mechanically. B discusses pollution. C does not address fatalities. D compares causes but does not answer whether speed limiters would reduce fatalities.

review.whyCorrect: A ties speeding deaths to the proposed intervention.
review.whyBIsWrong: B discusses fuel and pollution, not fatal accidents.
review.whyCIsWrong: C discusses choice, not whether fatalities would fall.
review.whyDIsWrong: D compares other causes without addressing the speed-limiter proposal.
review.whySafeToInclude: The fatal-accident aim is clear. Only A addresses it directly.
